followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the same basic verdict but slightly lower clarity/technical confidence in the presentation because the PDF confirms additional equation-level notation problems. I reject the other reviewers' fatal-truncation objection after checking the arXiv PDF/abstract: Section III exists there and the missing HTML values are 2.5-60 solar masses and 0.07. The central sigma-convention concern from my prior review remains the main technical issue.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: The claim that the manuscript is physically truncated and therefore unreviewable is not valid for the PDF version; it is an HTML rendering failure, not absence of Section III from the paper.
The missing mass-scale endpoints and missing Bayes factor in the supplied HTML are not substantive paper errors; the arXiv abstract/PDF give approximately 2.5-60 solar masses and B=0.07.
A technical-soundness score near 1 based solely on the broken HTML is over-penalized once the PDF is consulted.
The broad statement that the framework is technically sound because Section II is standard is too quick; Section III's sigma likelihood convention still needs mathematical checking.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The arXiv HTML bundle is genuinely broken at Section III; this is an accessibility/review-source problem, though not a fatal manuscript flaw when the PDF is available.
Concerns about Gaussian/independent-noise calibration under real detector noise, glitches, and correlated environmental noise are valid and should be addressed with diagnostics.
The SNR-selected 30-event sample and simplified selection treatment limit how broadly the population-level null result can be phrased.
The EFT/tidal power-law section is under-documented relative to the claim that current data cannot constrain the index.
The minor notation criticism about the CRISP integral dummy variable is valid; more importantly, the printed likelihood equation needs cleanup.

main_claims.claim: SCoRe/CRISP compresses waveform-subtracted residuals into cross-correlated detector power and supports Bayesian population inference over source-dependent deviations.
main_claims.notes: The conceptual structure is clear and mostly imported from earlier SCoRe papers. However, the printed CRISP likelihood is not fully clean, and the current manuscript relies heavily on prior derivations rather than re-establishing all assumptions.
main_claims.paper_location: Section II.A-II.C, especially CRISP definition, Bayesian hierarchy, and Wiener-filter statistic

main_claims.claim: The real-data analysis uses 30 GWTC-3 events selected by network matched-filter SNR above 12 and availability of strain data in both LIGO Hanford and Livingston.
main_claims.notes: The PDF gives the event table with component-mass MLEs, SNRs, and Wiener-filter statistics alpha. The text appears to refer to Table III, likely a typo for Table I.
main_claims.paper_location: Section III, paragraph before Section III.A; Table I

main_claims.claim: The unmodelled threshold search finds no evidence that low- and high-mass populations have different alpha variances, with Bayes factors 0.16-0.5 against the mass-threshold hypothesis and Savage-Dickey ratios 2-6 favoring equal populations.
main_claims.notes: The descriptive result is plausible from the reported alpha values, but the reported Bayes factors depend directly on whether sigma is the latent deviation scatter or the observed alpha scatter. The text, captions, and event likelihood point in different directions.
main_claims.paper_location: Section III.A, Figure 1 and surrounding text

main_claims.claim: The whole selected population is consistent with expected noise, with Bayes factor B_dev/nodev = 0.07.
main_claims.notes: The value 0.07 is present in the PDF and arXiv abstract, resolving the HTML omission. But the same sigma/noise-model convention issue affects whether the point null should be sigma=0 or sigma=1 and therefore how to interpret this Bayes factor.
main_claims.paper_location: Section III.A, final paragraph; Figure 3 caption; abstract

main_claims.claim: A modeled EFT/tidal-deformability power-law search is uninformative at current detector sensitivity, preventing inference of the power-law index.
main_claims.notes: The qualitative conclusion is credible and consistent with CRISP information loss, but the analysis is documented only briefly: no event-level likelihood plots, prior-sensitivity checks, or quantitative sensitivity curve are shown.

main_claims.claim: This is the first application of SCoRe to real GWTC-3 data.
main_claims.notes: The claim is plausible relative to the cited prior SCoRe simulation papers, but I did not independently exhaust the literature.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A precise convention for sigma and for the no-deviation/noise model in the unmodelled hierarchy.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The Bayes factors and the conclusion of consistency with noise depend on whether no deviation is sigma=0 with measurement variance 1, or sigma=1 as the observed alpha width.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The constant-in-time power template has adequate overlap with the unmodelled deviations relevant to the mass-threshold hypothesis.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III.A, choice of Z(t;theta) constant; Section II.C warning about template overlap
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A negative result is conditional on the sensitivity of this statistic, not a general exclusion of mass-dependent waveform deviations.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The selected 30 high-SNR H-L events are adequate for the stated population question over approximately 2.5-60 solar masses.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract; Section III event selection; Section III.A selection-effect discussion
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Mass, SNR, and detector availability are correlated; sparse low-mass or high-threshold splits can weaken or bias threshold Bayes factors.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Standard parameter estimation residuals have not absorbed or removed the deviations being tested.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section II.B source-parameter prior; Section III.A use of theta_MLE
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If waveform parameters absorb part of a physical deviation, residual-power tests can be biased toward the null.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Residual detector noise after subtraction has negligible cross-detector environmental correlations and nonstationary glitch contamination in the analyzed windows.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: SCoRe interprets coherent residual power as possible source-dependent deviation; unmodelled correlated noise could mimic or mask the signal.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: In the authors' intended generative model for Section III.A, should the no-deviation hypothesis be sigma=0 or sigma=1?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper's text/captions and likelihood formula point to different conventions; resolving this requires the authors' derivation or analysis code.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the Section II.A printed likelihood equation a typographical/rendering error, and what exact likelihood was used in the implementation?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The PDF-visible formula appears non-quadratic and dimensionally confusing, but a human should inspect the TeX/source/code before deciding whether it is only a typesetting problem.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do off-source or time-slide residuals validate the assumed unit-Gaussian alpha distribution for these 30 events?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper does not provide enough diagnostics to verify real-data calibration from the text alone.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How sensitive are the mass-threshold Bayes factors to marginalizing over component-mass posteriors instead of using m2,MLE?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The event table shows broad intervals, but recomputing the hierarchy requires posterior samples and the authors' pipeline.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Were detector-pair products implemented over unordered or ordered pairs?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The notation says I != J, while the two-detector table suggests one H-L statistic per event; code or author clarification is needed.

novelty_assessment: The novelty is moderate. The core SCoRe/CRISP methodology comes from earlier papers by the authors, but applying it to real GWTC-3 events and posing a simple mass-threshold population test is a useful empirical extension. The result is mainly a pipeline demonstration and null search; it is not field-shaping unless the statistical hierarchy and real-data calibration are made fully auditable.
score_rationale: The paper is a worthwhile real-data demonstration of an interesting prior framework, and the PDF resolves the other reviewers' strongest source-truncation objections. However, the main unmodelled Bayes factors are not cleanly auditable because the sigma/noise convention appears inconsistent, and the real-data noise calibration and threshold uncertainty are not sufficiently demonstrated. Novelty and significance are moderate, but technical soundness and clarity are pulled down by unresolved statistical and equation-level issues.
summary: The paper applies the prior SCoRe/CRISP framework to 30 high-SNR GWTC-3 events with Hanford-Livingston data, searching for mass-dependent cross-correlated residual power. After checking the arXiv PDF, I do not accept the strongest other-reviewer claim that the manuscript itself is unreviewably truncated; the HTML bundle is broken, but the PDF contains Section III and the missing abstract numbers. My main judgment remains that the project is useful but technically contested: the headline negative Bayes factors depend on an unresolved convention/likelihood issue for the unmodelled Gaussian width sigma, plus several calibration and threshold-classification assumptions. I do not claim to certify the full statistical pipeline or the underlying SCoRe derivations.

technical_objections.evidence: The text says the measured Wiener statistic alpha is drawn from Gaussian populations with standard deviations sigma1 and sigma2 and identifies the noise model as sigma1=sigma2=1. But the event likelihood after marginalization has variance sigma^2+1, which is appropriate if sigma is latent deviation scatter and the +1 is measurement/noise variance; under that model no deviation would normally be sigma=0, not sigma=1. If sigma is instead the observed alpha width, the +1 should not be present.
technical_objections.objection: The unmodelled hierarchy remains internally inconsistent about what sigma represents, which can change the headline Bayes factors.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III.A, prior pi(alpha|theta,Delta M), analytic event likelihood with sigma^2+1, Figure 1 caption, Figure 3 caption and final paragraph

technical_objections.evidence: The PDF text shows a log likelihood proportional to an integral of D^{IJ}(t) Z(t;beta) divided by <K_D(t)K_D(t)>, rather than a normal Gaussian quadratic such as a squared residual with a covariance/variance denominator or a clearly normalized matched-filter form. Since K_D itself is defined as an expectation of D(t)D(t), the extra expectation/product notation is also confusing.
technical_objections.objection: The printed CRISP likelihood formula appears mathematically incomplete or mistyped.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.A, likelihood equation after defining K_D

technical_objections.evidence: The actual unmodelled test compares zero-mean Gaussian variances of one statistic: total cross-correlated residual power obtained with a constant-in-time template over 30 selected events. Section II.C notes that alpha can miss deviations with poor template overlap. The analysis does not exclude mass-dependent deviations in mean, sign, phase/time structure, frequency structure, or effects producing equal variances in this statistic.
technical_objections.objection: The abstract and conclusion overstate the scope of the null result as disfavoring any mass scale in the catalog.

technical_objections.evidence: The threshold uses m2,MLE and sets the source-parameter prior to a delta function at the MLE. Several listed component-mass intervals are broad and can straddle plausible thresholds, so events near a threshold could move between populations. This matters because the Bayes factors are modest and some threshold splits are sparse.

The paper states that cross-bin correlations are negligible and later interprets high alpha events as likely statistical fluctuations, but it does not show off-source, time-slide, injection, or posterior predictive checks demonstrating the alpha distribution for these waveform-subtracted real detector segments after data-quality handling.
technical_objections.objection: The real-data calibration of alpha as independent unit-Gaussian noise is assumed more than demonstrated.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.A likelihood assumptions; Section III.A real-data analysis

technical_objections.evidence: The analysis sets xi independent of Delta M because events are high SNR and deviations are assumed too small to affect detection. However, the selected sample is SNR-limited and requires H-L strain data; the authors themselves note sparse low-mass bins and SNR selection effects near the lower mass thresholds.
technical_objections.objection: Selection effects are simplified in a way that limits population-level interpretation.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III.A, discussion of xi(Delta M) and high-SNR selection

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states that the posterior is uninformative in effective tidal deformability up to at least 10^5 for all events and therefore no population inference on the power-law index is possible, but it provides no event-level posterior plots, sensitivity estimates, or prior-dependence diagnostics for this part of the analysis.
technical_objections.objection: The modeled EFT/tidal search is too compressed to audit.

technical_objections.evidence: For a symmetric cross-correlation D^{IJ}=D^{JI}, a product over I != J counts ordered pairs. With only Hanford-Livingston data the table suggests one alpha per event, so this is probably notation rather than implementation, but the hierarchy should specify unordered detector pairs.
technical_objections.objection: The detector-pair product notation may double count symmetric pairs if read literally.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.B, product over detector indices I != J
